subject: Versatile weapons (longswords, warhammers, battleaxes, tridents, katanas)
method: full human/LLM read of transcripts 067 (Act 1) + 068 (Act 2/3), not the heuristic
note: All 40 tiers confirmed against the transcripts. TWO CORRECTIONS (swapped in stored data): the guide rates Voss's Silver Sword S when acquired in Act 1 and A in Act 3 — so 'Voss's Silver Sword' (Act 1) -> S and 'Voss's Silver Sword (Act 3)' -> A; the stored values were inverted. Corrected here and in tierlists.json. Auto-caption renderings: Larethian's Wrath='lorian's wrath', Phalar Aluve='Phalar/falo', Nyrulna='nyona'.

Larethian's Wrath.why: A +1 longsword that's also FINESSE (use Dexterity), with a once/rest 13ft-cone AOE that deals weapon damage (half on a miss); being finesse lets it go on builds that couldn't otherwise use a martial weapon — one of the best one-handers in Act 1.

Phalar Aluve.why: A +1 finesse longsword with two once/rest modes: Sing (stacks with Bless: +1d4 to your party's attacks/saves) and the broken Shriek (+1d4 thunder to enemies whenever they take ANY damage — fires several times per attack). Usable by any party; ~+40 damage/turn even unoptimized.

Voss's Silver Sword (Act 3).why: The same +2 longsword (+1d4 psychic, +1d4 attack/damage vs gith/aberration/fiend/elemental — a passive that helps even archers) but acquired in Act 3, where you have stronger default weapons — so a solid swap-in for those common Act 3 fights rather than a mainstay.

Charge-Bound Warhammer.why: A plain +1 warhammer, but bound by an Eldritch Knight / pact-of-the-blade warlock it becomes +2 with +1d6 lightning on every hit — best-in-slot melee damage for a bound-weapon character at the start of Act 2.

Dwarven Thrower.why: A +2 returning (homing) warhammer that, as a dwarf (or disguised as one), deals +1d8 (and +2d8 vs large+ enemies) — the best single-target throwing damage in the game; pair it with Nyrulna (use this for big single targets, that for AOE).

Nyrulna.why: A legendary +3 returning trident: +1d6 thunder, a 20ft thunder explosion on throw, +10ft move/jump and no fall damage, light, and two strong spells — incredible thrown OR as a one-handed weapon; a contender for best weapon in the game.

Trident of the Depths.why: Exploit-only +3 trident with +1d6 cold and an at-will Hailstrike (+1d10 cold, applies Chilled → freeze-stun with Wet) — enormous for a character WITHOUT extra attack (e.g. a Spore Druid), since the action-cost weapon action replaces the missing attack.

+1 Trident.why: Worth collecting because tridents are thrown weapons (full damage thrown, and a backup since returning weapons bug out when opening combat) AND it carries the unique Disarming Strike action — a powerful once/rest disarm on a stockpile-able weapon.

Faithbreaker.why: A +1 warhammer with FOUR short-rest weapon actions (so a good option nearly every turn), including one that doubles your strength-to-damage plus +1d6 force and a 17ft knockback — the best one-handed weapon for much of the early game.

Intransigent Warhammer.why: A no-enhancement warhammer that knocks all nearby enemies prone (spell-save DC) on a crit OR kill — superb on a crit-focused GOO warlock (prone + frightened = a one-hit stun) or a maim-build tiger barbarian; the passive lockdown outweighs the missing +1.

Azer Warhammer.why: Exploit-only no-enhancement warhammer with an at-will bonus action doing ~2d10 fire AOE (1d10 hit + 1d10/turn burning) in a 7ft radius — great for a melee AOE-fire character who runs in and explodes; worth giving up an enhancement bonus for.

Clown Hammer.why: A +2 warhammer where crits force a DC17 Tasha's Hideous Laughter (a 2-turn stun many late bosses like Raphael AREN'T immune to) — the catch (you can be laughed too) is nullified by the Clown Face Paint's free immunity; brutal on a crit/GOO build.

Duke Ravengard's Longsword.why: A +1 longsword granting +2 Charisma (up to 24); +2 Charisma is often worth a weapon slot by itself — great on a Charisma caster/sword-bard archer who doesn't otherwise need their melee weapon. (Its Commander's Strike is best ignored.)

Hammer of the Just.why: A +2 warhammer with +1d4 radiant (easy radiating-orb/reverb triggers) and +1d6 vs fiends/undead — a solid pick for radiant builds and for some of the game's hardest fiend/undead encounters.

Orphic Hammer.why: A legendary +3 warhammer with advantage on spell saves and an at-will action to remove restrained/paralyzed/stunned from an ally — a powerful 'un-stun' safety button (story-critical too) that can avert party wipes; defensive, so a support pick.

Voss's Silver Sword.why: Acquired in Act 1, this +2 longsword (+1d4 psychic, +1d4 attack/damage vs gith/aberration/fiend/elemental — about half the game's enemies, passive even for archers) is a standout that carries you through Acts 1-2 and into 3.

Witchbreaker.why: A +1 battleaxe granting advantage vs concentrating enemies (passive, works with spells/bows) plus a once/rest silence; good against enemy casters, but you should be preventing enemies from casting in the first place, so it comes up less than you'd think.

Blade of Oppressed Souls.why: A +1 longsword with +1d4 psychic and a once/rest Crown of Madness (Int save) that turns an enemy on its allies — situational and risky (the maddened foe targets the nearest creature, often you), but a fun, occasionally powerful effect.

Thermodynamo Axe.why: A +1 battleaxe that builds Heat on damage, discharged for bonus fire damage (and Cleave to spread it); only really worth it for a dedicated Heat-convergence AOE build — otherwise a default powerful weapon is better.

Blackguard's Sword.why: A +2 longsword with +1d4 necrotic that dazes on a Smite (DC13 con, easily passed); a fine passive bonus for a smiting paladin who wants to deny enemy reactions, but lower base damage than top Act 3 weapons and it doesn't work with Smite spells.

Sword of the Emperor.why: A +2 longsword with +1d4 vs shapeshifters and a flat +2 to saves vs spells; the +2 saves can make a character very spell-resilient (stacks with advantage), but it's a defensive effect on a slot you'd usually want for damage.

Trident of the Waves.why: A +1 trident that applies Wet and a water surface on hit — at-will Wet (double cold/lightning damage, freeze combos) is genuinely strong, but it doesn't trigger when thrown and lowers your damage, so it's a niche synergy weapon.

Vicious Battleaxe.why: A +2 battleaxe adding a flat +7 on any crit (works from the unequipped slot, stacks with Dolor Amarus for +14) — decent as an off-hand crit stat stick on a dual-wielder archer, but most crit builds prefer extra damage dice or crit chance.

Longsword.why: Non-magical d8/d10 with great action economy (Rush Attack to dash+attack, bonus-action Pummel Strike daze); the Fighter's starting weapon and fine very early, but you'll upgrade to a magical weapon ASAP.

Trident.why: Non-magical, lower damage (d6/d8) but thrown, and crucially the only weapon with the Disarming Strike action — a very powerful once/rest disarm that makes the base trident worth using in some early fights.

Warhammer.why: Non-magical d8/d10 with arguably the best base action set (Concussive Smash daze, Weakening Strike, Back Breaker prone); the Paladin's starting weapon and a strong early chassis until you find a magical one.

+1 Warhammer.why: The best of the generic +1 versatile weapons and a reasonable early-merchant buy when you can't find a Morning Star or a good two-hander; you'll swap it out for Phalar Aluve or another magical weapon soon.

The Sparky Points.why: A no-enhancement trident generating lightning charges on hit (effectively a +1 trident plus a little extra damage), but it costs you the quest choice of the much more useful Spell Sparkler — so rarely worth it.

Cruel Sting.why: A +1 longsword with a once/rest Ensnaring Strands (web) and +1d4 poison vs restrained enemies; the spell is a weak level-1 effect by Act 2, and bonus damage against an already-locked-down enemy doesn't accomplish much — niche.

Battleaxe.why: Non-magical; a solid chassis (Cleave, Maiming Strike for an on-demand stun combo), but by the time you'd pick one up you're upgrading to magical weapons anyway.

+1 Battleaxe.why: A generic +1 battleaxe — one-handed users prefer a Morning Star and two-handed users want a greatweapon, so the plain +1 has no niche.

+1 Longsword.why: A generic +1 longsword; outclassed by the Morning Star (one hand) or a greatweapon (two hands), with no special effect to justify it.

Adamantine Longsword.why: Ignores slashing resistance and auto-crits objects, but it costs an adamantine forge far better spent on armor/shields — and the adamantine scimitar/sickle does the resistance trick as a light weapon, so this is never the pick.

Ceremonial Battleaxe.why: A story-puzzle item that's otherwise just a generic +1 battleaxe acquired late — no special properties, so D for weapon use.

Ceremonial Longsword.why: Like the other ceremonial weapons: a generic +1 longsword for a story puzzle, outclassed by what you already have.

Ceremonial Warhammer.why: A generic +1 warhammer used in a story puzzle; no combat reason to use it over your existing weapons.

Githyanki Longsword.why: Appears as a non-magical or +1 longsword identical to the generic versions, and only after you already have those — so there's never a need for it.

Ketheric's Warhammer.why: A +1 warhammer with +1d4 psychic and no other effect; it's available in the same place as the almost-strictly-better Blade of Oppressed Souls, so there's no reason to use it.

+2 Longsword.why: A generic +2 longsword; by the time it appears you have far better magical options, so it's skippable.

Katana.why: A reskinned +1 longsword (an Easter-egg nod to the famous 'katana = +1 longsword' copypasta) — no special properties, D in Act 3.
